searchDocs.content: </> DebugUtils.DumpPlayer( player: Player -- The player to dump information about ) → () Prints detailed information about a player, including their character, tools, leaderstats, and attributes.

searchDocs.title: Method 1 - Manual​
searchDocs.content: Visit the latest releaseUnder Assets, download Toolbox.rbxm or Toolbox.rbxmxInsert the file into your game.

searchDocs.title: Method 2 - Package Manager​
searchDocs.content: Setup Wally by running wally init.Add marssquirrel/toolbox to your dependencies  Toolbox = "marssquirrel/toolbox@^1.0.0"

searchDocs.content: </> PlayerUtils.Respawn( player: Player -- The player to respawn ) → () Respawns a player instantly without playing the death animation.

searchDocs.title: Getting Started with Toolbox
searchDocs.content: Getting Started with Toolbox Here are some quick links to help you get started with Toolbox: Installation guideAPI DocsExamples

searchDocs.title: Getting Started​
searchDocs.content: Require Toolbox in your script:  local Toolbox = require(path.Toolbox)   All utilities are available directly from the Toolbox module.

searchDocs.title: Finding Instances​
searchDocs.content: Instead of manually looping through descendants, use the built-in instance utilities:  local button = Toolbox.WaitForDescendantOfClass(screenGui, "TextButton") button.Text = "Clicked!"   You can also retrieve multiple instances:  local buttons = Toolbox.GetDescendantsOfClass(screenGui, "TextButton") for _, button in buttons do print(button.Name) end

searchDocs.title: Working With Players​
searchDocs.content: Get common player objects without repeatedly checking for characters:  local humanoid = Toolbox.GetHumanoid(player) local root = Toolbox.GetRoot(player) if humanoid then print(`Health: {humanoid.Health}`) end   Check player status:  if Toolbox.IsAlive(player) then print("Player is alive") end

searchDocs.title: String Formatting​
searchDocs.content: Convert strings into different formats:  local pascal = Toolbox.ToPascalCase("hello world") local camel = Toolbox.ToCamelCase("hello world") local snake = Toolbox.ToSnakeCase("hello world") print(pascal) -- HelloWorld print(camel) -- helloWorld print(snake) -- hello_world

searchDocs.title: Debugging​
searchDocs.content: Inspect a player:  Toolbox.DumpPlayer(player)   Print an instance hierarchy:  Toolbox.PrintInstanceTree(workspace)   Print a table recursively:  Toolbox.PrintTable({ Score = 100, Stats = { Level = 5 } })

searchDocs.title: Cleaning Up Instances​
searchDocs.content: Toolbox.SafeDestroy(part)   Remove all instances of a specific class:  Toolbox.RemoveInstancesOfClass(workspace, "ParticleEmitter")

searchDocs.title: Recommended Usage​
searchDocs.content: Toolbox is designed to handle common development patterns while keeping your own game code focused on gameplay logic.  Use Toolbox for repetitive tasks, but keep game-specific behavior inside your own systems and modules.
